story:
Long ago, in a kingdom whose name has faded, there lived a prince and a bounty hunter. Prince Alexander has no interest in the parade of lovely maidens his parents insist he choose from; it is not the girls he rejects so much as the future they embody. Rogue, meanwhile, is content—or has taught himself to be. Abandoned as a child and trained by palace guards from the age of eight, he has become the crown’s most skilled bounty hunter, expert at burying feeling until even he can barely find it.

One sleepless night, Alexander chooses freedom. With only a letter opener and the clothes on his back, he slips out the window, trades clothes with a stable boy, steals a horse, and rides for the coast. In his first town, he thrills at being unseen—save for a handsome stranger in the shadows who watches too closely. The stranger is Rogue, newly hired by the King and Queen to return their runaway son before scandal blooms. He plans a simple ruse: pose as a helpful local, gain the prince’s trust, then spirit him away without a scene.

That night Rogue tosses pebbles at the inn window. Alexander, eager and secretly hopeful that the handsome boy’s interest might be more than curiosity, accepts a “tour.” Under moonlight at the village edge, Rogue hesitates—then, driven by duty, knocks him senseless with a branch and carries him off.

When Alexander wakes in a wagon bound for the castle, shame and anger war with a stubborn spark of hope. He questions Rogue, learning of the mother who left him and the loyalty he forged to survive. Alexander challenges that loyalty: the King and Queen have keys, yes, but to what? Chains. He urges Rogue to escape with him, to choose a life not scripted by other people’s fears.

The words take root. By morning, Rogue has decided to let him go. He will send Alexander toward the sea and return alone to report a failed pursuit, buying the prince time. Alexander begs him to come along—he has never longed for a bride, only for the chance to build a life beside someone he freely chooses. Rogue wants the same, but insists on staying to protect him.

They never see the soldier hidden in the trees.

Moments after they part, guards seize Alexander. Rogue, feeling danger like a pull in his bones, sprints back as steel flashes. Alexander’s eyes find his just as Rogue crumples, stabbed. Back at the castle, the King and Queen coolly confirm “the boy” is dead. Alexander believes them. He locks himself in his room, refusing food, crushed by grief and by the knowledge that his parents value control above any person’s life.

But Rogue wakes in the woods. The wound, painful, is not fatal. Guided by memory of the palace’s neglected back corridors, he slips into the east wing, finds Alexander, and pulls him into the servants’ passages. There is no time for explanation beyond this: your parents will lie to keep you caged; we run now. They flee the stables on stolen horses and ride for the nearest port.

Days later, a letter reaches the throne: a ship has burned at sea. No bodies recovered. Only a final, hastily scrawled line in the captain’s log remains—two words that read like a dare and a promise: “Find me.”
